speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4, the interview with Nathan Krieger. The date is December 4, 1995. You were telling me about the [GERMAN] who gave you the salamis. Tell me a little bit more about him. He helped you in other ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- when [INAUDIBLE] took me in as a sick person there, into the [NON-ENGLISH], and I told him that I don't mind working, but we have so much work. And we try not to give you every second day. We try to give you every day. I have a brother which was a very good tailor and everything. And he came with me, but they send me to [INAUDIBLE]. Could you find out where he was sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came back to me. He told me that he that he was sent to [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a gold mine-- a coal mine. It was-- I knew nobody comes out from there. It was horrible work. So I said to him, could you help me something? So he came back and he told me, and he said he doesn't have time this week, but in next week or week later, he's going to go there and see if he could get him out. He promised me. And he went there, and he came back. He didn't say anything to me. So I says to him, [GERMAN], you know, you promised me something that you're going to do for me. He looked at me, and he said, I did. I said, what do you mean by that? He said, I was too late. On April the 24, 1944, they took your brother to the crematory.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] If he would be dead, he would bring it. He would do it, because he said to me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --you need me now, but I'm going to need you after the war. That was exactly his words. And he was-- he was very nice. He-- whatever we asked him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then when they took us from Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we do that, before we go forward from Auschwitz, let's spend a little more time there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When you asked the [GERMAN] to go look for your brother, what time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been after April 24. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much afterwards? Much afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't much after it, because the reason I remember the dates, because that was on my birthday. That was February 21. I was there until April 21-- March 21. And then it took a while until-- you know, we got-- until, you know, it's a thing. I just walked into the kitchen. I saw him with a dirty jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you had been working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember exactly. But you know, this is a coincidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if I wouldn't come in-- you know, they say you have to be in the right time, in the right place. You know, I was in the wrong place, but in the right time. And it must have been two months later, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other activities going on that were trying to help each other, trying to do things? Resistance, sabotage, other things going on that you were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my friend. Not-- I wish I would meet him when I came in there, because he came in-- his name was Yusef Wolf. But he was in charge-- he was in charge in the underground. He was a writer before the war. And he also was a writer after the war. He wrote a lot of books. And I met him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: --while I was already in the [NON-ENGLISH], while I had it very good. I mean, comparing-- I had-- I had food and I had everything. I was-- I was very well off. And he told me that he has connections to the camp where the women are. They work for munition by Hess-- ammunition factory there. And we have a friend, which she's alive. She lives in Los Angeles, in Israel. Bertha Braunhood is her name. And she worked in the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: The what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A [GERMAN] is in the office where they kept all the names, you know. And he told me that he got in touch with them, and that my wife is there. So I said, give them a message to tell my wife that I'm alive, that I'm in Brunnlitz, and that I am OK, that I have everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was involved in other activities that you knew about?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, but I didn't-- I didn't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any activities that you did know about, like when you went out with those two salamis? Were there are other things people were doing, hiding food or trying to get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no food there to hide. There was only five people, and I was the sixth. And there were [NON-ENGLISH], what they had food. And the people what worked mostly were Polish people working in the kitchen, you know, but whoever worked in the kitchen had food. But-- --I'll go back to 19, before, to Wisnicz Nowy. I was privileged to have a blessing from the Belzer rebbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go often to visit? You told me your family was religious. Did you often go to visit this rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father was a [NON-ENGLISH]. There was a dynasty, [NON-ENGLISH], and he was a [NON-ENGLISH] as well. But the Belzer rebbe came in to Wisnicz from wherever he was, because it was that time, Wisnicz, a lot of people from Krakow, from all over, they came because we lived in the houses. And let's say whoever had the house with two rooms or three rooms had to take in-- you know, if you had three rooms, you had to take in eight people, for instance. I give a number. But, you know, it has to be three, four-- three, four persons in the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1942, because in Krakow, the ghetto was already in '41. So people run away from there. They came to Wisnicz. In Bochnia, the ghetto was, so they run away from there. They came-- [INAUDIBLE] not to live in the ghetto, so they came to Wisnicz. And the Belzer rebbe came, and I was-- I had the privilege to come to get a blessing from him. And I remember like today. I have to repeat the words. He asked me, [INAUDIBLE] So in Europe they called me Nute Krieger. So I said, Nute Krieger. He says no, no, no, no. [NON-ENGLISH]. So I said Nute Krieger. So he says no, no, [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you call you to the-- I said [NON-ENGLISH]. And I told him that. He always used to sit like this. He never looked anybody. He picked up his head, and he said [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, God will help you. You're going to live through the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What should I tell you? From 1939 to 1945, if I wasn't dead a million times--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --and so many times happened to me. I was on the death march. That's another story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't walk anymore. And I says, how could that happen to me? I said, if you couldn't walk, if you couldn't get up, they came with the machine guns and [INAUDIBLE] and you were finished. So the Belzer rebbe gave me a blessing that I'm going to live through, and I'm a believer. I believe in God.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, how could that happen? All of a sudden, like a miracle, I got my strength, got up, and the group was already-- I don't remember how many yards or whatever. I couldn't get up to join them to walk, but I got the strength to get up and to run and to join them. And how-- what they got up later and run, they shoot him. How didn't? Because the Belzer rebbe gave me a blessing. And you have to believe in it. That's the message that I have for my grandchildren and my kids. They never leave hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't lose hope, because God helped me. He'll help you too. What happened? When did you leave the [NON-ENGLISH]? When did you leave the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January the 18, when they start to evacuate Auschwitz, when the Russians came and closed. So by January the 18, everybody had to go out. People-- they took them on trains. Some people walked. And I told that SS man I want to make a bunker. He says no, no, no. He said, wherever I go, you guys go with me. He said, I'm taking you six guys. And don't worry about it. I'm going to watch over you. The war is coming to an end. And I helped you. And I'm going to need your help after the war. Because he said he joined the SS, not that he wanted. He was forced to it. He was really-- he was a nice-- nice human being. I wish there would be more like him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got lost. As they took us on the train, the partisans came, and they started to shoot. And then the Russian-- I mean the German army came, and they told us to run out from the wagon, and from the cars. And we lost them. And I lost my other four friends-- five friends what I worked with them. But I remained with my friend Joe Kuchler, which lives now in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were talking, what we're going to do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I lost this guy. It's going to be terrible. So we went ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a forest not too far away, and we run in there, and we were hiding there. We were there a whole night. And in the morning, the Germans came in with the German shepherds. And they started to pull out the people. And the people were screaming and yelling. It was terrible. And they went on the loudspeakers. They was not going to join. [NON-ENGLISH] was not going to join. He says it's going to be shot. It's going to be-- eat up alive. We had no other choice. We went ahead and we joined the group. And this is how the death march started.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were on this march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thousands, I think. I was told that it was 14,000 to 15,000 on that march when we started up. We walked from Poland to Germany to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked out January the 18 from Auschwitz. Schindler came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on March 30, something like that, 29, because I know I was about five weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, by Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that, on the march. You were on the march first.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on the march.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: From January 18 to March the 29, 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about the march. Did people die along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Terrible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think by March 30, we were 150 people out-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --of God knows how many thousands. What happened when people died? They just left them there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever couldn't walk, they shot him. That's it. My friend from Toronto helped me a lot. Without him, I wouldn't be around. I helped him. He helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food on this march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you eat? How did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: With snow. If you walked into-- walked through a town, if the mayor was a nice guy, he used to cook some soup. More people died going, getting the soup, than to live without the piece of soup. In the beginning, I was strong, so I carried a hop sack for a German, for the SS men, so he used to give me a piece of bread. Or when they cooked some soup, so I used to tell them, I don't want to go there. So he tried, and he brought me a little bit of soup there. In the beginning, I had a pair of shoes. And then they took out-- they took a German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --prisoner who was a big shot, with the red [INAUDIBLE] that means political prisoner. So he took off my shoes, because I had a good pair of shoes, because I worked in the [NON-ENGLISH]. So for food, I could get a good pair of shoes. But they took it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked 20, 30 below 0. If they shoot a guy, they took off his [INAUDIBLE], his uniform. Everybody took a piece of [NON-ENGLISH], wrapped around our legs, and this is how we walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they put them in the movie theater. In Europe was 200, 300 people, that was already a big theater. They put in 2,000 or 3,000 because the SS wanted to go to sleep. So they piled up 2,000, 3,000 people in a church, a movie theater. And whoever was strong enough survived. Whoever was underneath never survived. Whoever was on top survived, because they put them in a small room like this, a few thousand people, where normally 200 people went in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the march. And I could talk about it for now-- they put us once into a tunnel, but there was no light on the end of the tunnel. The tunnel didn't go through. Hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of people died there. I was looking-- I remember like the day I was looking for something sharp to cut my wrist. And the last thing that I remember, I put my head down. It was water. And I was lucky there was water, a little bit, so my head went there. I fall asleep. And in the morning, I woke up, so I heard they were yelling. They were pulling out from people there. There were more dead people there than would come out from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people try to escape during this march? Did people try to leave the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? In Poland? We had nowhere. In Germany? Where are you going to escape? Whoever escaped, they brought them back. They shot him. You're going to escape, where are you're going to go? Right away, you were a target. You wore the uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Remember he was marching, [INAUDIBLE], so they're bringing out food for the dogs. I said, guard-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --over there, dog gets hot food because it was cold, and the steam came out from the-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I always ask myself the same question. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was lucky. Again, 30 people. That number 30 must be-- Schindler find out that they're flapping around, dead people. So he came in and he said to the SS that whatever-- his, the factory, he needs people to work, that he wants to take all the people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember they negotiated forth and back and this and that. So they agreed he's going to give him only 30 people. The rest, the SS were afraid if they're going to give everybody up, they're going to take them on the front lines. So this way they had the job, to watch-- to watch dead people.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, I was lucky that my friend Joe, he said, let's stay here. Let's stay there. So we were lucky that we were picked between-- Did you know that this was a good thing to be picked? Did you know Schindler--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything was better. Anything was better. Even they would pick us to the crematorium was better than to go to ahead and to work, frozen, without food, without clothing. It was cold. In Europe it is cold. In Europe it's-- and it's already-- whatever strength I got in the [NON-ENGLISH] that [INAUDIBLE], was gone already. It already-- it's January the 18 until March 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I sit at night, I can't sleep, and I ask myself a question. How could a human being take so much punishment? Could you imagine without food, without-- you should excuse me. We didn't wash from January 18 until March the 30. You went with your hand like this, and you picked the lice from your body.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Without food, without washing, without anything. And I sit, and I ask myself how-- Auschwitz already was good. You had a bath. You came after a day, you laid down, whatever. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That march was terrible. And the Belzer rebbe gave me a blessing that I'll live through. So Schindler came. I come out, and he saved, you know, 30 people. And I was lucky to be in the 30 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us on trains. They gave us bread right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where this was that you were sent, that you was taken from the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know where we were. We walked. We marched. Who knew where we were? Like animals, whatever-- whatever they ask you to do, you had to do it. If you didn't want to do it, you were finished. That's it. So we came, and I came into Schindler there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brunnlitz. And the first person I saw was my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Weiner. And I said, Henry, give me something to eat. I'm starving. I'm dead. He said, he can't. I can come over. They're going to take you and undress you, take you to the unlousing, and disinfect, wash you and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the trip like?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, don't worry about it. You are here already. You are safe. Here you're going to live through the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had he been there, your brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the trip like from the death march to Schindler's camp in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was good already. We were on a train, and the civilians used to come in and give us food and everything. We weren't already under-- we were under his jurisdiction. We weren't already-- because he took us. So over there, once we came on the station-- and the Czechs are beautiful people. They're very nice people. Everybody-- whoever had food took it out and gave it to us and everything. And we smelled it already. That's already a change. That is something for the good. We didn't worry about it, like going to other trains, to Auschwitz or different places, [INAUDIBLE]. We knew already that this is already something good. We came there as-- we met a lot of guys that I knew. And I knew my brother-in-law, and he says, don't worry about it. You're here. And I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. My brother-in-law, a friend of his from his town, which they came from the Bochnia ghetto, and his father, when they were the [NON-ENGLISH] in the Bochnia ghetto, so I knew him. So he says to me, Nathan, if you ever see my children, you're going to be the last one to give my regards. And I was the last one.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came there, I said to Henry, is Romek [INAUDIBLE] here? And he said yes. So he said to me, you know what happened? I said his father sends regards, because when they send me here, he went-- he was an older person. He went to the crematorium. And I remember back to that. So he said to me, come. You're going to work with me. I said, fine. He says, what are you doing? He was in charge in the factory on the [NON-ENGLISH]. That means to black out the windows, that airplane comes, he shouldn't see any lights or whatever. So he gave me his box with the tools. There was a hammer there and everything. And I said to him, Romek, I can't pick it up. I don't have the strength for it. What am I going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, don't worry about it. I'm going to work for you. He said, you're going to be in charge, and I'm going to carry the tools. Such a nice guy. Then we met in Germany after the war, and then he lived in [? New Haven. ?] He used to come. I used to go there. He used to come here. His sister was gone already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anything happen while you in Schindler's camp? Did anything happen? Did any Nazis or SS come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE] I went over there, didn't worry anymore. [INAUDIBLE] Because I didn't do anything. I was-- I walked around with him to watch that everything should be OK. And I had food already because my brother-in-law helped me. And he helped me with food and everything. So I didn't worry over there. And one day a guy comes in and he said there are no more guards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was liberated May the 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see what happened to Schindler, where he went? Did you see Schindler before he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I was still-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say one thing-- couldn't think straight, couldn't act straight-- weak physically, mentally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there for a while, and then I started to head home, and met up with my wife. I came back to Bochnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back to Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, walking, trains. You did everything, because the railroads were bombed. The bridges were bombed and everything, so we went from Czechoslovakia. We pushed ourselves with my friend Joe, which we kept together. And we went to Bochnia. I came to Bochnia, and I was there for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get from Czechoslovakia to Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now for a tape change. We'll continue on the next tape.
